title: ## STANDARD FOR QUICK-FROZEN RASPBERRIES CXS 69-1981 Adopted in 1981. Amended in 2019, 2022.

sections.section: ## STANDARD FOR QUICK-FROZEN RASPBERRIES CXS 69-1981 Adopted in 1981. Amended in 2019, 2022.
sections.summary: Document title: 'Standard for Quick‑Frozen Raspberries CXS 69‑1981', adopted 1981 and amended in 2019 and 2022 (title/metadata, not substantive requirements).
Documentation note that following decisions at the Forty-fifth Session of the Codex Alimentarius Commission (Dec 2022) amendments were made to Section 6.4 (Labelling of non-retail containers).
Editorial correction replacing the misspelling in Section 3.2.2 so it reads 'refractometer at 20 °C' instead of 'refractomer at 20 °C'.

Scope: this standard applies to quick-frozen raspberries (Rubus idaeus L.) offered for direct consumption without further processing (repacking permitted) and does not apply when the product is indicated as intended for further processing or other industrial purposes.

Describes the product (quick-frozen raspberries from Rubus idaeus L.), the freezing process requirement (range of maximum crystallization must be passed quickly and the product must reach −18 °C at the thermal centre after stabilization), permits controlled repacking, requires handling to maintain quality during transport/storage/distribution, and allows presentation as free‑flowing or non‑free‑flowing.
Defines quick-frozen raspberries as product prepared from fresh, clean, sound, ripe, stemmed raspberries of firm texture conforming to Rubus idaeus L. (red, yellow or black varieties).

Specifies the freezing process: product must be frozen so the range of maximum crystallization is passed quickly and quick‑freezing is not complete until the thermal centre reaches −18 °C after stabilization; the recognized practice of repacking under controlled conditions is permitted.

Requires that the product be handled to maintain quality through transportation, storage and distribution up to final sale and recommends following the Code of Practice for the Processing and Handling of Quick‑Frozen Foods (CXC 8‑1976).
States presentation styles: quick‑frozen raspberries may be offered as free‑flowing (individual berries not adhering) or non‑free‑flowing (solid block).

Sets composition and quality framework: optional sugars are permitted; soluble solids limits and test method are specified for products prepared with dry sugars (18–35% m/m) or syrup (15–30% m/m) with syrup amount limited to that required to cover berries; defines 'defective' for composition and requires lot acceptance by sampling plan with AQL 6.5, and establishes quality factors including general requirements, mineral impurity limit (≤0.05% m/m), free‑flowing tolerance (10% m/m), a 300 g standard sample unit, visual defect scoring with allowable point totals and a disintegration limit (max 35% m/m), plus lot acceptance rules for quality criteria (AQL 6.5).
Permits optional sweeteners: sugars including sucrose, invert sugar, invert sugar syrup, dextrose, fructose, glucose syrup and dried glucose syrup.

Specifies compositional limits: for dry sugars the thawed‑sample soluble solids must be 18–35% m/m (as sucrose) and for syrup the syrup may only cover/fill between berries with soluble solids 15–30% m/m (as sucrose); samples outside these ranges (with up to a 5% excess allowance) are 'defective' and lots are accepted by an appropriate sampling plan with AQL 6.5.

Requires that the total soluble solids of liquid from a thawed, comminuted sample for raspberries prepared with dry sugars be between 18% and 35% m/m expressed as sucrose, determined by refractometer at 20 °C.

For raspberries prepared with syrup, permits only enough syrup to cover berries and fill spaces and requires total soluble solids of the thawed, comminuted sample to be between 15% and 30% m/m expressed as sucrose, determined by refractometer at 20 °C.

Defines a 'defective' for composition as any sample unit falling outside the soluble solids limits in 3.2.1 or 3.2.2 provided it does not exceed those limits by more than five percentage points of soluble solids.

States that a lot meets compositional criteria when the number of 'defectives' does not exceed the acceptance number (c) of an appropriate sampling plan with an AQL of 6.5 (see Codex methods on analysis and sampling).

Details quality factors: general appearance and tolerance requirements; analytical limits including mineral impurities ≤0.05% m/m and free‑flowing tolerance 10% m/m; definitions and scoring for visual defects with a 300 g sample unit and specified allowable point totals and a disintegration limit of 35% m/m; defines 'defective' for quality criteria and prescribes lot acceptance by a sampling plan with AQL 6.5, treating 'free‑flowing' defects individually in addition to other allowances.

Sets general quality requirements: quick‑frozen raspberries must have good, reasonably uniform colour characteristic of the variety, be clean, sound and free from foreign flavour/odour, and tolerances are specified for sand/grit, adhered berries, uncoloured or completely uncoloured berries, stalks, extraneous vegetable matter, blemishes, development, varietal similarity and disintegration.

Defines tolerances for visual defects using a 300 g drained‑berry sample unit with defects assigned points per Table 1 and maximum 'Total Allowable Points' for categories (minor, major, serious, total) and notes disintegrated/not intact limit of maximum 35% m/m.

Defines a 'defective' for quality criteria as any sample unit (per an appropriate sampling plan with AQL 6.5) that exceeds tolerances for mineral impurities (3.3.2), free‑flowing (3.3.3), the Total Allowable Points for visual defects (3.3.6) or the disintegrated tolerance in Table 1 (3.3.6).

States that a lot is acceptable for quality criteria when the number of 'defectives' (per 3.3.7) does not exceed the acceptance number (c) of an appropriate sampling plan with AQL 6.5, and that a 'defective' for 'free‑flowing' is treated individually in addition to other allowances.
Food additives: none permitted (prohibition).

Hygiene: recommends preparing and handling the product in accordance with the General Principles of Food Hygiene and relevant Codex codes of practice, requires the product to be free from objectionable matter, and when tested by appropriate methods to be free from microorganisms, parasites, and microbial-origin substances present in amounts that may represent a hazard to health.

Labelling: states that, in addition to the General Standard for the Labelling of Pre-packaged Foods, specific provisions apply and are set out in subsections 6.1–6.4 (name of the food, additional requirements, bulk packs, non‑retail containers).

Name of the food: the label must include the word "raspberries" and the words "quick-frozen" (or "frozen" where customary), and must indicate colour for non-red varieties and the packing medium (e.g. name of sweetener and form).
Additional labelling requirements: packages must bear clear directions for keeping (storage) from purchase to use and directions for thawing.

Bulk packs: for quick-frozen raspberries in bulk, required information may be on the container or in accompanying documents, but the name of the food with "quick-frozen" (or "frozen" as allowed) and the manufacturer/packer name and address must appear on the container.
Labelling of non-retail containers: labelling should follow the General Standard for the Labelling of Non-Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346-2021).

Packaging: packaging for quick-frozen raspberries must protect organoleptic and other quality characteristics, guard against microbiological and other contamination, prevent dehydration/heat accumulation/leakage as practicable, and not impart any foreign odour, taste, colour or characteristic during processing and distribution up to final sale.

Methods of analysis and sampling: compliance with the standard must be checked using the methods of analysis and sampling contained in the Recommended Methods of Analysis and Sampling (CXS 234-1999) relevant to the standard's provisions.
